operations.rationale: Retired after the semantic-representation chapter folded into Compact Generative Systems on 2026-06-30; the semantic-leasing material now lives in canonical destination prose.
content_lines: The semantic-node lifecycle is a rule for how much trust the stack may place in a representation. A node may begin as proposed, which means it can support search, discussion, and triage but not operational reliance. It can become grounded when active provenance exists. It can become adequate for a declared task when the required constraints survive representation. It can become interoperable only for named consumers that have actually consumed it without hidden translation loss.

The lifecycle also has negative and historical states. A superseded node remains available for lineage and migration, not fresh use. A stale node requires review because its provenance, hierarchy, or consumer assumptions may have changed. A quarantined node is misleading, ungrounded for its claimed use, or contradicted, so it cannot be used operationally except for debugging.

That lifecycle makes semantic graphs editable without becoming forgetful. Updating a node should preserve the old reference or record supersession, because downstream claims may still depend on the earlier meaning.

operations.rationale: Retired after the semantic-representation chapter folded into Compact Generative Systems on 2026-06-30; the destination chapter now carries semantic-node consumer policy in canonical prose.
content_lines: Semantic structure is carried by the Semantic Node Record. Its minimum vocabulary is deliberately boring: node identity, concept label, provenance references, parent and child references, relation references, tokenization contract, grounding state, version, supersession links, residual uncertainty, permitted uses, and evaluation references. The point is not to make the record impressive. The point is to make later use inspectable.

Different consumers need different boundaries. VCM needs adequacy state, source coverage, and a loss contract before a node becomes a semantic page. Spinoza and the claim ledger need provenance, support tier, defeaters, and a downgrade path before a semantic claim can participate in belief revision. Planning needs permitted uses, residual uncertainty, and authority limits before it can safely plan through a node.

Cognitive compilation needs typed inputs, outputs, constraints, and repair behavior before it can lower the node into IR. Compression needs utility and fallback records for the represented task before a compact token can be treated as cheaper. Human readers need an explanation trace and visible non-claims, because a clean concept path can otherwise look more settled than the evidence allows.

That interface makes semantic representation a shared boundary rather than a private model feature. A node may be useful for routing while still disallowed for proof, citation, audit, or claim support. The representation earns trust only when the consuming layer can see what was preserved, what was dropped, and where to fall back.
